title: STANDARD FOR YAM CXS 340-2020 Adopted in 2020.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR YAM CXS 340-2020 Adopted in 2020.
sections.summary: Title/header indicating the Standard for Yam (CXS 340-2020) and that it was adopted in 2020.

Classifies yams into three classes: 'Extra' (superior quality, essentially defect‑free except very slight superficial defects not exceeding 5% of surface and very slight shape defects), Class I (good quality allowing slight defects not exceeding 10% of surface and a slight shape defect), and Class II (produce not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimums, allowing defects up to 15% of surface); in all classes defects must not affect the pulp or impair quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Defines the 'Extra' class for yams as superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free from defects except for very slight superficial defects (non‑transversal cuts/surfaces or scars up to 5% of the surface) and very slight shape defects, provided these do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I yams must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type; slight defects are allowed only if they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping or presentation — specifically non‑transversal cuts/scars up to 10% of the surface and slight shape defects — and defects must not affect the pulp.

Class II covers yams that do not qualify for higher classes but meet minimum requirements, permitting defects (e.g., non‑transversal cuts/scars up to 15% of surface and shape defects) provided the yam retains essential quality, keeping and presentation characteristics and defects do not affect the pulp.

Yams may be sized by weight or existing trade practices (the latter must be labelled with size and method); when sized by weight, size is based on individual tuber weight or package weight range, and an optional guide table gives size codes A–J with corresponding weight ranges.

At all marketing stages tolerances are allowed and non‑conforming produce may be re‑sorted per CXG 47‑2003; permitted quality tolerances are: 'Extra' class up to 5% (including 1% decay and 1% dirt), Class I up to 10% (including 2% decay and 1% dirt), Class II up to 10% (including 2% decay and 1% dirt); size tolerances (if sized) are 10% by number or weight.

Tolerances at all marketing stages allow re‑sorting per CXG 47‑2003, with specified class‑by‑class allowances: Extra class 5% non‑conforming (including 1% decay and 1% dirt), Class I 10% non‑conforming (including 2% decay and 1% dirt), and Class II 10% non‑conforming (including 2% decay and 1% dirt).
'Extra' class: up to 5% by number or weight may fail the class requirements but meet Class I, including allowances of 1% for decay/soft rot/internal breakdown and 1% for dirt/impurities.
Class I: up to 10% by number or weight may fail the class requirements but meet Class II, including allowances of 2% for decay/soft rot/internal breakdown and 1% for dirt/impurities.
Class II: up to 10% by number or weight of yams in a lot may fail the class requirements, including allowances of 2% for decay/soft rot/internal breakdown and 1% for dirt/impurities.
Size tolerances: for all classes, if produce is sized, up to 10% by number or weight may not meet the indicated size.

Presentation requirements: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, though distinctly different colours may be mixed if uniform in quality and origin per colour) and the visible contents must represent the whole; packaging must protect produce using clean, food‑grade, non‑damaging materials (printing with non‑toxic ink allowed), comply with CXC 44‑1995, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards with no foreign matter or smells.

Each package must be uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, though differently coloured yams may be mixed if uniform by colour and origin, and the visible portion must be representative of the entire contents.

Packaging must protect yams using clean, food‑grade materials that do not cause damage, with printed materials allowed only if non‑toxic, and packing must comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44‑1995).
Containers must provide necessary quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance for handling, shipping and preserving yams, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Labelling requirements supplement the General Standard (CXS 1‑1985): consumer and non‑retail packages must show the name of produce, origin, and may show variety/commercial type; non‑retail packages must bear legible export/packer identification and commercial specifications (class, size as range/code/method, optional cooking type) visible on the same side or on accompanying/accessible electronic documentation for bulk consignments, with an optional official inspection mark.
Consumer packages must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) plus the specific labelling provisions set out for yams.
Each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce as 'yam' and may additionally indicate the variety(ies) and/or commercial type.

Origin labelling: the country of origin must be declared (optionally district or place), and for mixtures of distinctly different varieties from different origins the country of origin must be shown next to each variety's name.

Non‑retail containers must bear specified particulars legibly, indelibly and visibly on the same side; for bulk transport these particulars must appear on an accompanying document attached visibly inside the vehicle or be provided as an accessible machine‑readable electronic document.
Identification: non‑retail packages must state the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher and may include an optional identification code.
Non‑retail name of produce: packages must state the name of the produce and may state the variety and/or commercial type.

Requires the country of origin (and optionally district or national/regional/local place name); for mixtures of distinctly different yam varieties or species from different origins, each country's origin must be indicated next to the respective variety or species name.
Commercial specifications must indicate class and size (expressed as a size range in kg, a size code per the table in Section 4, or by the method used); cooking type is optional.
Only a heading noting an optional official inspection mark; no substantive requirements are provided in this section.
States that the produce must comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and with the maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with Codex hygienic practice texts (e.g., CXC 1-1969, CXC 53-2003) and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.
